title: Eating Disorders Are More Than a Feminist Issue: A Conversation with Amber Husain
description:
“What they refuse to consider is that neuroses concerning food might be driven by meaningful factors beyond pressures on vanity (assumed to be feminine).”

The post Eating Disorders Are More Than a Feminist Issue: A Conversation with Amber Husain appeared first on Public Books.
hook: Food neuroses aren't just about looking good—they're about what our bodies are allowed to want and need in the first place.

title: Fieldwork As a Sex Object by Meena Kandasamy review – story of a deepfake sex tape
description:
The author of When I Hit You returns with a pithy, savagely funny tale of online shaming and the Indian manosphere

We can all agree that the internet today, especially two particular platforms owned by the world’s greatest megalomaniacs, is a hellscape. But if you think X and Facebook are purgatories of friendless trolls endlessly posting hate and bullying women, each other and minorities under the guise of free speech, wait till you experience the Indian version of that netherworld, as
hook: A woman's deepfake sex tape goes viral in India's manosphere. Her response is pithy, savage, and genuinely funny.

title: Poetry from the Factory Floor
hook: A conversation with a poet who writes from the factory floor—labor, language, and what work does to the voice.

title: ‘Spider-Noir’ Review: Nicolas Cage Slays in Pulpy, Multicolor ‘Spider-Man’ Spinoff
description:
Spider-Noir never undercuts its sense of atmosphere with a knowing wink.

The post ‘Spider-Noir’ Review: Nicolas Cage Slays in Pulpy, Multicolor ‘Spider-Man’ Spinoff appeared first on
hook: Nicolas Cage in noir pulp that never winks at itself—atmosphere as the entire point, not irony as a crutch.

title: The In-Between Worlds of Larissa Borteh
description: In the artist's paintings, are we looking at plants in a state of beautiful decay, ghosts, deities, fairylands, or something from a dream?
hook: Larissa Borteh paints things in states of decay so beautiful you can't tell if you're looking at death or transcendence.
